Why Did We Help Each Other?

Explaining Peer Support in Learning Social Networks Theory and Methods

Adam Talsma

December 2021

Introduction

This social networks class involved online interaction among students and teachers as a key part of the learning process. We used Moodle then Slack to seek out and provide help in learning networks and R. Our teachers often helped, but we students also provided responses and helped each other. This project seeks to determine what explains who helped who, and considers likely network and non-network factors as possible determinants.

Theory

Education scholars theorize peers as effective co-constructors of learning (Vygotsky, 1978). Empirical studies have shown that online collaboration platforms, including Slack, can support peer learning in response to individual queries among graduate students (Walker et al., 2021. Some prior studies contend that graduate students give knowledge in learning communities for four reasons: altruism, social trust, belonging (Brouwer and Jansen, 2019), and cultural integration (Sadykova 2014). In contrast to the literature to date, I propose that the evolving structure of the digital interactions between students may also explain their decisions to help.

Data

For this project, I mined our class’s digital footprints in Moodle and Slack that were accessible to all of us. I built a peer support network of directed ties for each time a student helped another student. I then surveyed all 24 student to gather theoretically-motivated network attributes, and to build a network of offline relationships at two points in time: before the class and part-way through.

Hypotheses

The three network hypotheses that I introduce as my main predictor variables are:

  • \(H_1\): In-degree Centrality: (rate) the more students receive help from peers, the more likely they are to “pass it on” by helping others.

  • \(H_2\): Out-degree Centrality: (rate) The more students help, the more likely they are to keep helping.

I test my hypotheses with proxies for the theoretical controls that the literature has identified.

  • Altruism: Students that are more altruistic help other students more.

  • Social Trust: Students who send more offline social ties will help more.

  • Belonging: Students who have been at the Institute longer will help more.

  • Culture: Students in their cultural environment will help more. I consider Local/International, European/Non-European and Northern/Southern.

I also considered a few additional control variables:

  • R Knowledge: Students who know R will help more.

  • Difference in Learning style: Students that prefer group-work will help more.

  • Gender: Ambivalent, arguments for either gender helping more.

  • Popularity: People with more in-degree ties will help online less.

Note that I ruled out all variables that were the same across the class. For example, everyone knew that their engagement on Moodle/Slack would factor into their final grade.

Network Visualization and Attribute Statistics

Let’s start off with a few attribute statistics on our class.

Descriptive Statistics of Students in Our Class (N = 24)
Statistic female yearsIHEID altruist collaborative knowR european
Mean 0.542 1.542 0.792 0.583 0.625 0.458
St. Dev. 0.509 0.588 0.415 0.504 0.495 0.509

The first graph is the online student helps network near the end of the class with nodes sized by out-degree centrality (how many people you’ve helped).

  • The size of the nodes in Fig. 1 reveal significant variations in how many people we help.
  • Europeans and Men seem to be helping more.

The second graph is the social networks around the same time with nodes sized by out-degree centrality as well (how many people you socialize with). The attribute and network patterns are very different from the social network in Fig 2: the size of the nodes varies less, and many of the students that are on the fringes of Fig. 1 are centrally located in Fig. 2 and vice versa.

Modelling & Findings

To test my hypotheses, I modeled the network with DyNAMs using Goldfish. I selected DyNAMs because my helps data consists of events time-stamped by the minute. My project is about explaining why we help, so I focus on DyNAMs rate models, which DyNAMs models separately from the choice function. The modeling was done using backwards selection. I used “window effects” to evaluate whether more recent interactions were explaining the help network, settling on a 45 minute window.

The table below compares the results from the four model I built. They show consistent results with my predictions, although there were some surprises.

*** p < 0.001; ** p < 0.01; * p < 0.05.

Key takeaways:

  • There is strong support for (\(H_2\)).

  • I find negative support for \(H_1\).

  • Curiously, we find little evidence that any other peer support predictors theorized by the literature help explain who helps. In fact, we even find altruism results in a negative associations.

  • We find strong support of positive associations between being European and male.

  • Neither popularity, nor preferring group work, nor knowledge of R had a statistically significant relationship—nor any other variables drawn from the social relationships network.

  • Finally, there is significant window effect, as a few network centrality effects that otherwise didn’t show an association, became significant predictors within a 45 minute window.

An analysis of the waiting time (between helps to peers) reinforces these findings. Across all students the average time between helps was 44 days. For a non-European woman that has helped 3 others already their time between helps drops to 30 hours. However, for a European male, their expected response time is predicted to be 19 hours.

I conclude the network analysis by selecting the model with the lowest AIC: Model 4. DyNAMs have yet to develop accessible diagnostics. I proceed to the Conclusion.

Model AIC Comparison
AIC
Model 1: Full 3734
Model 2: Trim 3734
Model 3: Full Window 3678
Model 4: Trim Window 3665

Conclusion

Network effects do have explanatory power for peer support online, as do attributes including gender and culture. Surprisingly, we found that, if anything, altruism, knowledge of R, social interactions offline, and preference for working in groups were negatively associated with helping others. My hunch is that the structure of online threads may play a significant role in shaping this helps network. Future research could explore this further. Hopefully this report demonstrates that social networks theory and DyNAMs could be be a part of such an undertaking.
